messages.content: You are a Lean 4 autoformalization agent working in a current Mathlib-only project. You must formalize the provided TeX/math source chunk into one Lean theorem or lemma, including a proof, using only the Lean prefix context provided. The target Lean statement, target declaration name, and original proof are intentionally withheld. Avoid stale Lean 3/old Mathlib syntax and identifiers; follow the lean_environment/current_version_guidance and local Lean examples. Return exactly one JSON object.

{
  "task": "Formalize the source chunk as one Lean theorem/lemma and prove it.",
  "required_json_schema": {
    "lean_declaration": "one complete Lean theorem or lemma, including proof/body; do not include imports or markdown",
    "declaration_name": "the local name you gave that theorem/lemma",
    "used_context": [
      "short list of source/context facts used"
    ],
    "notes": [
      "brief caveats, if any"
    ]
  },
  "instructions": [
    "Do not use sorry, admit, aesop? placeholders, or comments standing in for proof.",
    "Do not include import statements; the generated file already imports Mathlib.",
    "Do not assume access to the withheld target Lean statement or name.",
    "For multi-part TeX chunks, formalize only the specified labeled part/source span. Do not conjoin all parts unless the record explicitly asks for the whole multi-part result.",
    "Do not cite or invent raw helper names that are not present in the Lean prefix context/local examples; prefer displayed local style and standard Mathlib APIs.",
    "Do not redeclare definitions, structures, abbrevs, notation helpers, or instances already present in the Lean prefix context; reference them directly.",
    "Do not introduce theorem-local `where` definitions or redefine concepts such as summability, limits, binomial coefficients, or matrix operations. If a needed definition is not in context, state a narrower theorem using the displayed APIs.",
    "Use current Lean 4/Mathlib syntax and API names; if your memory conflicts with displayed local context, trust the displayed context.",
    "State a single proposition-level theorem/lemma that is likely to match the specified source part; do not bundle typeclass instances or unrelated source parts into conjunctions.",
    "Every nonstandard helper theorem or local API used in the proof should appear explicitly in the Lean prefix context or local examples. If it is not displayed, do not use its name.",
    "If an existing theorem or lemma in the prefix context has binders, apply it to the needed variables rather than using the theorem constant bare.",
    "Prefer the narrowest theorem directly supported by the source sentence; avoid generalizing to a stronger forall/if statement unless that exact shape is in the source or prefix context.",
    "Prefer a short proof if the prefix context already contains the needed fact."
  ],
  "context": {
    "source_statement_or_chunk": [
      {
        "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/tex/FPS/LaurentSeries.tex",
        "line_range": [
          225,
          313
        ],
        "original_line_range": [
          226,
          286
        ],
        "line_range_adjustment_reasons": [
          "expanded_backward_to_include_environment_begin",
          "expanded_forward_to_close_environment"
        ],
        "labels": [
          "def.fps.laure.double"
        ],
        "snippet": "\\begin{definition}\n\\label{def.fps.laure.double}Let $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]  $\nbe the $K$-module $K^{\\mathbb{Z}}$ of all families $\\left(  a_{n}\\right)\n_{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}=\\left(  \\ldots,a_{-2},a_{-1},a_{0},a_{1},a_{2}%\n,\\ldots\\right)  $ of elements of $K$. Its addition and its scaling are defined\nentrywise:%\n\\begin{align*}\n\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}+\\left(  b_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\n\\mathbb{Z}}  &  =\\left(  a_{n}+b_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}};\\\\\n\\lambda\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}  &  =\\left(  \\lambda\na_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\text{for each }\\lambda\\in\nK.\n\\end{align*}\nAn element of $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]  $ will be called a\n\\emph{doubly infinite power series}. This name is justified by the fact that\nwe will later use the notation $\\sum_{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}a_{n}x^{n}$ for a family\n$\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}\\in K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]\n\\right]  $.\n\\end{definition}\n\nNow, let us try to define a multiplication on this $K$-module $K\\left[\n\\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]  $, in order to turn it into a $K$-algebra\n(like $K\\left[  \\left[  x\\right]  \\right]  $). This multiplication should\nsatisfy%\n\\[\n\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}\\cdot\\left(  b_{n}\\right)\n_{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}=\\left(  c_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}%\n,\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\text{where}\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ c_{n}=\\sum_{i\\in\n\\mathbb{Z}}a_{i}b_{n-i}%\n\\]\n(since this is what we would get if we expanded $\\left(  \\sum_{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}%\n}a_{n}x^{n}\\right)  \\left(  \\sum_{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}b_{n}x^{n}\\right)  $ and\ncombined like powers of $x$). Unfortunately, the sum $\\sum_{i\\in\\mathbb{Z}%\n}a_{i}b_{n-i}$ is now infinite (unlike for $K\\left[  \\left[  x\\right]\n\\right]  $), and is not always well-defined. Thus, the product $\\left(\na_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}\\cdot\\left(  b_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}$\nof two elements of $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]  $ does not\nalways exist\\footnote{The simplest example for this is $\\left(  1\\right)\n_{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}\\cdot\\left(  1\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}$. If this product\nexisted, then it should be $\\left(  c_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}$, where\n$c_{n}=\\sum_{i\\in\\mathbb{Z}}1\\cdot1$, but the latter sum clearly does not\nexist.}. Therefore, the $K$-module $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]\n$ is not a $K$-algebra. This explains why our above computations have led us astray.\n\n\\subsubsection{Laurent polynomials}\n\nIf we cannot multiply two arbitrary elements of $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm\n}\\right]  \\right]  $, can we perhaps restrict ourselves to a smaller\n$K$-submodule of $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]  $ whose elements\ncan be multiplied?\n\nOne such submodule is $K\\left[  \\left[  x\\right]  \\right]  $, which is\nembedded in $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]  $ in the\n\\textquotedblleft obvious\\textquotedblright\\ way (by identifying each FPS\n$\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{N}}$ with the doubly infinite power\nseries $\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}$, where we set $a_{n}:=0$ for\nall $n<0$). But there are also some others. Here is one:\\footnote{For proofs\nof the statements made (implicitly and explicitly) in the following (or at\nleast for the less obvious among these proofs), we refer to Section\n\\ref{sec.details.gf.laure}.}\n\n\\begin{definition}\n\\label{def.fps.laure.laupol}Let $K\\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  $ be the\n$K$-submodule of $K\\left[  \\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  \\right]  $ consisting of\nall \\textbf{essentially finite} families $\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\n\\mathbb{Z}}$. This is indeed a $K$-submodule (check it!). It should be thought\nof as an analogue of the ring of polynomials $K\\left[  x\\right]  $, but now\nallowing for negative powers of $x$.\n\nThe elements of $K\\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  $ are called \\emph{Laurent\npolynomials} in the indeterminate $x$ over $K$.\n\nWe define a multiplication on $K\\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  $ by setting%\n\\[\n\\left(  a_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}\\cdot\\left(  b_{n}\\right)\n_{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}=\\left(  c_{n}\\right)  _{n\\in\\mathbb{Z}}%\n,\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\text{where}\\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ \\ c_{n}=\\sum_{i\\in\n\\mathbb{Z}}a_{i}b_{n-i}.\n\\]\nNote that the sum $\\sum_{i\\in\\mathbb{Z}}a_{i}b_{n-i}$ is now well-defined,\nbecause it is essentially finite.\n\nWe define an element $x\\in K\\left[  x^{\\pm}\\right]  $ by%\n\\[\nx=\\left(  \\delta_{i,1}\\right)  _{i\\in\\mathbb{Z}}%\n\\]\n(where we are using the Kronecker delta notation again, as in Definition\n\\ref{def.kron-delta}).\n\\end{definition}\n"
      }
    ],

        ],

        "path": "AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean",
        "line_range": [
          845,
          853
        ],
        "text": "Any doubly infinite power series f equals (coeff f n) at position n.\nThis is the pointwise characterization of the notation ∑_{n∈ℤ} a_n x^n:\nat each position m, the coefficient is a_m.\n\nThis formalizes the representation mentioned at the end of Definition `def.fps.laure.double`:\n\"we will later use the notation ∑_{n∈ℤ} a_n x^n for a family (a_n)_{n∈ℤ}\".\n\nNote: This is a formal identity of families. The \"sum\" is well-defined because\nat each coefficient position m, only the term n = m contributes.",
        "policy": "source-facing Lean doc comment only; target Lean declaration name and statement remain withheld"
      },
      "target_comment_labels_or_parts": [],
      "context_mode": "target-comment",
      "instruction": "If the source snippet contains multiple labeled or numbered parts, formalize only the specific part/source span indicated by specific_source_labels, specific_labeled_parts, or target_declaration_source_comment. Do not conjoin all parts unless the record labels identify the whole multi-part result. The target_declaration_source_comment is source-facing prose only; it is not the hidden Lean target statement."
    },
    "context_mode": "target-comment",
    "available_imports": [
      "Mathlib"
    ],
    "lean_prefix_context": "-- File context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:41-41 (open)\nopen scoped LaurentPolynomial\n\n-- File context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:45-45 (namespace)\nnamespace AlgebraicCombinatorics\n\n-- Predecessor context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:772-790\n/-- The K-module of doubly infinite power series K[[x^±]].\n\nAn element is a family `(a_n)_{n ∈ ℤ}` of elements of K. Addition and scalar\nmultiplication are defined entrywise.\n\nNote: This is NOT a ring because multiplication is not always well-defined\n(the convolution sum may be infinite).\n\nThis corresponds to Definition `def.fps.laure.double`.\n-/\ndef DoublyInfinitePowerSeries (K : Type*) [Semiring K] := ℤ → K\n\nnamespace DoublyInfinitePowerSeries\n\nvariable {K : Type*} [Semiring K]\n\ninstance : AddCommMonoid (DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) := Pi.addCommMonoid\n\ninstance : Module K (DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) := Pi.module _ _ _\n\n-- File context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:784-784 (namespace)\nnamespace DoublyInfinitePowerSeries\n\n-- File context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:786-786 (variable)\nvariable {K : Type*} [Semiring K]\n\n-- Predecessor context: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:792-793\n/-- The coefficient of x^n in a doubly infinite power series -/\ndef coeff (f : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) (n : ℤ) : K := f n\n-- Local API retrieval: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:808-809 (single)\n/-- The element x^n in K[[x^±]], represented as a family -/\ndef single (n : ℤ) : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K := fun m => if m = n then 1 else 0\n-- Local API retrieval: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:811-813 (coeff_single)\n@[simp]\ntheorem coeff_single (n m : ℤ) : coeff (single n : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) m =\n    if m = n then 1 else 0 := rfl\n-- Local API retrieval: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:817-818 (coeff_zero)\n@[simp]\ntheorem coeff_zero (n : ℤ) : coeff (0 : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) n = 0 := rfl\n-- Local API retrieval: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:820-822 (single_zero_eq_one)\n/-- The single at 0 with coefficient 1 represents 1 (as a constant series). -/\n@[simp]\ntheorem single_zero_eq_one : (single 0 : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) = fun n => if n = 0 then 1 else 0 := rfl\n-- Local API retrieval: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:824-827 (coeff_single_self)\n/-- Coefficient of single n at n is 1. -/\n@[simp]\ntheorem coeff_single_self (n : ℤ) : coeff (single n : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) n = 1 := by\n  simp [coeff_single]\n-- Local API retrieval: AlgebraicCombinatorics/LaurentSeries.lean:829-833 (coeff_single_ne)\n/-- Coefficient of single n at m ≠ n is 0. -/\n@[simp]\ntheorem coeff_single_ne {n m : ℤ} (h : m ≠ n) :\n    coeff (single n : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) m = 0 := by\n  simp [coeff_single, h]",
    "lean_environment": {
      "toolchain": "leanprover/lean4:v4.28.0",

      "current_version_guidance": [
        "Generated code is checked with the repository lean-toolchain and current Mathlib, not Lean 3 or an older Lean 4 snapshot.",
        "Prefer Lean 4 syntax: `fun x => ...`, `by` tactic blocks, namespaces/dot notation as shown in the prompt, and current Mathlib names from displayed context.",

        "For power-series coefficients, follow the local examples' argument order such as `coeff n (X : R⟦X⟧)`, not `coeff (X : R⟦X⟧) n`.",
        "For Laurent polynomial samples, prefer displayed current APIs such as `LaurentPolynomial.T n` and local notation `K[T;T⁻¹]`; do not use bare polynomial `X` for Laurent polynomials.",
        "Do not make typeclass objects (`CommRing α`, `Algebra R A`, etc.) components of an `∧`; those are types/classes, not propositions. Put them as assumptions/instances or use theorem statements about terms instead.",
        "If the source theorem is a narrow identity, formalize that identity directly rather than a broad bundled theorem whose components will not match the grader's withheld statement."
      ],

      ]

    "local_lean_style": {
      "guidance": [
        "Match the local Lean style shown in local_lean_style.examples when it applies; prefer exact APIs and argument order already used in this file.",
        "Use only identifiers that appear in the Lean prefix context/local examples or are standard Mathlib identifiers; do not cite or invent raw helper names from guessed notation expansions.",
        "If the local examples use a domain-specific API such as `Ring.choose`, `updateCol`, or a local theorem name, keep that exact API family instead of falling back to a more familiar but different API such as `Nat.choose` or `Matrix.updateColumn`."

        "/-- Coefficient of single n at n is 1. -/\n@[simp]\ntheorem coeff_single_self (n : ℤ) : coeff (single n : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) n = 1 := by\n  simp [coeff_single]",
        "/-- The single at 0 with coefficient 1 represents 1 (as a constant series). -/\n@[simp]\ntheorem single_zero_eq_one : (single 0 : DoublyInfinitePowerSeries K) = fun n => if n = 0 then 1 else 0 := rfl",
